title: The shard your biggest customer melted
tagline: Three shards loaf at 15%. The fourth just OOM-killed Postgres. Your largest partner says hi.
brief: You run partner telemetry ingest: an edge LB fronts an ingest API that writes every event to a 4-shard Postgres, hash-partitioned on user_id. Yesterday your biggest partner went live — 40,000 devices, all authenticating as ONE service account. Total traffic is 6,000 events/s against 12,000 writes/s of aggregate capacity. The cluster should be coasting at 50%. Shard-2 has other plans.
symptom: Open the Postgres node card and watch the shard distribution: ~78% of writes hash to shard-2 — 4,680/s into a shard that can write 3,000/s — while the other three idle near 440/s. Shard-2's memory pegs at 100% within seconds and the whole database CRASHes with memory exhausted, even though aggregate utilization never left 50%.

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Append-heavy telemetry store on a 4-shard Postgres. No partitionKey declared in the schema — the effective shard key comes from the database node's own config.shardKey.

diagnosis.question: Aggregate write load is 6,000/s against 12,000/s of write capacity — 50% — yet the database OOMs within seconds, every run. What's the root cause?

options.text: The shard key is user_id — every event from the dominant service account hashes to the same shard, so one shard carries most of the load.
options.explain: Right. A hash shard key spreads KEYS evenly, not LOAD — 40,000 devices behind one user_id all hash to shard-2. 78% of 6,000/s is 4,680/s into a shard whose write capacity is 12,000 ÷ 4 = 3,000/s: 156% utilization, memory pressure, OOM. The three idle siblings prove capacity was never the problem; placement was.

options.text: The cluster is undersized — four shards can't absorb 6,000 writes/s, so add shards or a bigger box.
options.explain: The run's own numbers rule this out: 6,000/s against 12,000/s aggregate write throughput is 50% utilization, and three of the four shards sit near 440/s — about 15% of their slice. Re-sharding to 8 while keeping user_id just re-hashes the dominant account onto ONE of the new shards: the hot spot moves, it doesn't shrink.

options.text: The connection pool is exhausted — queries queue behind the 200-connection limit and back up into memory.
options.explain: Pool exhaustion has a signature this run never shows: activeConnections climbing toward the pool limit and a 'connection pool exhaustion' log with failing queries. Here activeConnections stays near zero because the database drains its inbound every tick — and the failure is shard-2 memory, which a bigger pool wouldn't touch.

options.text: Inserts are slow because the events table is unindexed, so every write triggers a full-table scan that bloats memory.
options.explain: A scan problem surfaces as latency, not as memory on exactly one shard — and p99 stays in the tens of milliseconds right up to the crash. The events table also carries a btree on event_id. A missing index can't explain why one of four identical shards dies while its siblings idle: that asymmetry is a placement problem.

fix.objective: Pick a shard key that spreads LOAD, not just keys: survive the full 6,000 events/s partner launch with every shard cool, nothing crashing, and zero failed writes.

criteria.label: Zero failed writes at the database

hints: The Postgres node card shows the split: shard-2 takes ~78% of writes while the other three idle. Which config field decides which shard a row lands on?
shardKey is user_id, and your launch partner's 40,000 devices all share one user_id — every event hashes to the same shard: 0.78 × 6,000/s = 4,680/s into a shard that can write 12,000 ÷ 4 = 3,000/s.
Re-key on something unique per ROW, not per customer: set shardKey to event_id. The same 6,000/s then spreads to 1,500/s per shard — 50% of each shard's write capacity, with 2× headroom.
